metadata.working_title: Boosting biodiversity monitoring using smartphone-driven, rapidly accumulating community-sourced data

abstract_sketch.problem: Comprehensive biodiversity data is crucial for conservation planning, protected-area designation (e.g., KM-GBF 30by30), and nature-related financial disclosure, yet traditional expert surveys cannot supply the required spatiotemporal resolution.
abstract_sketch.gap: Community-sourced observations accumulate rapidly but typically suffer from spatial and taxonomic biases and variable identification accuracy, limiting their utility for species distribution modelling, particularly for endangered and underrepresented taxa.
abstract_sketch.approach: We evaluate 'Biome', a Japan-deployed smartphone app that combines AI-assisted species identification with gamification, and test whether blending Biome records (at 50% share) with traditional survey data improves MaxEnt species distribution models (SDMs) for 132 terrestrial plants and animals.
abstract_sketch.key_results: Biome accumulated 5,275,457 records (40,957 species) by 7 July 2023; species-level identification accuracy exceeded 95% for birds, reptiles, mammals, and amphibians and was 90% for seed plants; Biome+Traditional SDMs reached Boyce index > 0.9 with 294 plus-or-minus 471 records versus 2,129 plus-or-minus 4,157 records for Traditional-only models, and 338 plus-or-minus 571 versus 2,336 plus-or-minus 3,718 for endangered species.
abstract_sketch.impact: Smartphone-driven community science, when combined with statistical safeguards on sampling effort, can meaningfully support 30by30 conservation planning, OECM designation, and corporate TNFD disclosures, especially for data-deficient endangered species.

sections.title: Introduction
content_bullets: Biodiversity underpins ecosystem services that are declining rapidly, motivating KM-GBF goals including 30by30 and TNFD-aligned corporate disclosure.
Conservation planning requires high-resolution species distribution knowledge, but expert surveys cannot scale.
Community-sourced platforms (eBird, iNaturalist) already contribute to phenology, invasion tracking, trait mapping, and distribution modelling, but their data are spatially biased and presence-only.
Gamified mobile applications can broaden participation beyond expert volunteers and accelerate data accumulation.
We introduce Biome, a Japan-deployed app launched in 2019 that amassed 6 million records by 17 October 2023, roughly four times the GBIF-sourced Japanese total over the same period.
MaxEnt SDMs can exploit presence-only records while correcting for sampling effort via pseudo-absence selection.
Paper contributions: (i) quantify Biome data quality across taxa and species rarity, and (ii) compare SDM accuracy for 132 species using Traditional versus Biome+Traditional training data.

sections.title: Related Work
content_bullets: Community science platforms and their ecological applications (eBird, iNaturalist).
Quality assessment of citizen-science observations (e.g., volunteer invasive-plant surveys, 72% correct; plant-ID app benchmarks, 69%).
Statistical treatment of presence-only data: MaxEnt and sampling-effort correction for pseudo-absences.
Prior work combining traditional and community-sourced data for SDMs, mostly focused on birds.
Gamification and engagement in citizen science.

subsections.title: The Biome application and record filtering
content_bullets: Biome, launched April 2019, downloaded 839,844 times by 13 September 2023.
Image + geolocation + timestamp ingestion; CNN-based image recognition refined by local species-frequency priors.
Ask-Biomers identification requests and gamified points/levels, with rare/endangered/invasive species weighted higher.

Invalid-record filtering: user reports, private records, non-wild labels, cultural-centre and pet-store locations, certified-user gating (<15% misidentification rate, <0.5% inappropriate records, >20 public records).

subsections.title: Assessing record accuracy
content_bullets: 1,420 records audited across 8 taxonomic groups.
Rare species defined as <=10 occurrences; common species as top 15% of records per taxon.
145 records per rare/common class for insects and seed plants; 70 for other taxa.
Experts reidentified photographs; correctness scored at species, genus, and family levels.

subsections.title: Species distribution modelling
content_bullets: MaxEnt implemented via ENMeval 2.0 on R 4.1.3.

Training datasets: Traditional survey (GBIF, NCRE, Forest Ecosystem Diversity Basic Survey, curated literature, post-1970 records cleaned with CoordinateCleaner) and Biome+Traditional (Biome fixed at 50%).

Predictors: land use, bioclim, vegetation, lithology, elevational range, biogeographic-region categoricals (Blakiston's Line, oceanic-island indicator), linear + quadratic + hinge features, regularisation multiplier 2.5.
10,000 pseudo-absences per model selected with picking probabilities proportional to sampling effort; two complementary schemes combined for Biome+Traditional.

Test-set construction: 25% Biome + 75% Traditional, intermediate to 0% vs. 50% training mixtures; Biome test records accepted only when >=99% estimated posterior true presence under multi-user co-observation.
Central Japan region (33.7-37.7N, 136.2-137.6E) used as spatial block.
Boyce index computed on test species with >=50 test occurrences (132 species).
Downsampling sweep from 20 to 20,000 training records with three iterations per condition (12,351 runs total).

subsections.title: Statistical comparison
content_bullets: Per-species, per-data-size mean Boyce index across three iterations, Fisher z-transformed.
Generalised linear mixed model in lme4/lmerTest with fixed effects for data type, log(amount of records), and their interaction, and species-level random intercepts.

subsections.title: Amount and environmental coverage of Biome data
content_bullets: 5,275,457 records of 40,957 species by 7 July 2023; ~5,407 submissions/day on average in 2022.
PC1 of environmental variables accounts for 6.1% of total variation and separates Biome (urban-natural uniform) from Traditional (natural-biased) data.
Taxonomic mix: seed plants 41.8%, insects 31.2% of records.
After automatic filtering, 2,373,303 records (45.0%) retained for analysis.

subsections.title: Identification quality across taxa and rarity
content_bullets: Wild-individual fraction >97% for insects and birds; <90% for molluscs, seed plants, mammals, fishes.
Species-level identification >95% in birds, reptiles, mammals, amphibians; <90% in insects, fishes, seed plants.
Genus-level accuracy >90% for all taxa except insects; fishes and seed plants gain 5-6% moving from species to genus.
Family-level accuracy >94% across all examined taxa.
Common vs. rare species: 95% vs. 87% average identification accuracy.

subsections.title: SDM accuracy with Biome-blended training data
content_bullets: Intercept of Boyce Index did not differ between datasets (beta=0.02 plus-or-minus 0.03, t=0.60, P=0.55).
Slope of BI on log(records) higher for Biome+Traditional (beta=0.02 plus-or-minus 0.01, t=3.72, P<0.001).
BI > 0.9 reached with 294 plus-or-minus 471 records for Biome+Traditional vs. 2,129 plus-or-minus 4,157 for Traditional.
Endangered species: 338 plus-or-minus 571 vs. 2,336 plus-or-minus 3,718 records for the same threshold.
Best attainable BI per species equivalent across datasets (0.81 plus-or-minus 0.20 vs. 0.83 plus-or-minus 0.20), reflecting capped data volume under the 50% constraint.
Sensitivity analysis shows 50-70% Biome blends yield the highest relative improvement (Appendix 1).

sections.title: Discussion
content_bullets: Biome's identification accuracy (90% for seed plants) compares favourably with published plant-ID app benchmarks (69%) and volunteer invasive-plant reports (72%).
Two mechanisms explain the SDM improvement: (i) greater environmental coverage of urban-natural gradients and (ii) increased training volume when the 50% constraint is relaxed.
For endangered species, Biome data reduces the data requirement for accurate SDMs by roughly an order of magnitude, making community data especially valuable where expert records are sparse.
Seed plants remain the hardest taxon due to mixed wild/non-wild status and limited identification traits; certified-user filtering and multi-image submissions could help.
Limitations: spatial-only transferability test, limited data for some taxa (molluscs, amphibians, reptiles, mammals), and presence-only outputs that are not absolute presence probabilities.
Future directions: OECM and protected-area prioritisation, invasion and phenology tracking, time-series analysis using two-step ML frameworks, and integration into TNFD-aligned corporate disclosures.
